coredata.dc:title: Development of various carbon nanoparticles and albumin complexes for potential theranostics applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Yazdani, Maedeh
Rahmandoust, Moones
Kouchakzadeh, Hasan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are promising materials for many biomedical applications. However, their unique properties may be much enhanced by heteroatom doping and albumin decorating, which leads to improved functionality as reliable CQD-based simultaneous diagnosis and drug delivery systems, respectively. Therefore, in this study, three types of CQDs, with various doping conditions, namely pure carbon dot (CDs), N-doped CQD (NCDs), and S–N doped CQD (SNCDs), were functionalized over bovine serum albumin (BSA), a type of serum protein that is commonly used in drug delivery systems, and their binding was systematically optimized using the central composite design algorithm, by response surface methodology. Therefore three types of CQD-BSA complexes were obtained, namely CD-BSA, NCD-BSA, and SNCD-BSA. The statistical system was set to achieve the best results for both the absorbance of BSA, as well as the emission strength for each CQD-BSA complex. The structural changes of BSA have been investigated by the FTIR spectrum for various CQD-BSA complexes, indicating that the secondary structure and biological activity of BSA have not been influenced significantly, after incubation with different CQDs, except for a slight change in the case of SNCDs-BSA complex, inferring non-covalent interaction in some binding sites. The fluorescence intensity results also show that the formation of the complex did not reduce the emission of CQDs very much. Based on the results, the synthesized complexes can serve as suitable carriers for simultaneous drug delivery and diagnosis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Binding interaction
Bovine serum albumin
Biocompatibility
Nanocomposites

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM and DLS of the synthesized CQDs (a,b): CDs, (c,d): NCDs, (e,f): SNCDs and (g,h): BSA, respectively.
From left to right: UV–Vis Spectroscopy, XRD patterns and FTIR spectrum of the synthesized CQDs (a): CDs (b): NCDs and (c): SNCDs; and (d) the FTIR spectrum of BSA.
From top-left to down-right, the full, C-1s, O-1s, N-1s, and S-2p XPS spectrum of (a): CDs, (b): NCDs, and (c): SNCDs.
Main effects of NCDs and BSA concentration on (a): emission intensity (%), and (b): absorbance in the CDs-BSA complex.
Main effects of NCDs and BSA concentration on (a): emission intensity (%), and (b): absorbance in the NCDs-BSA complex.
Main effects of NCDs and BSA concentration on (a): emission intensity (%), and (b): absorbance in the SNCDs-BSA complex.
From top to bottom TEM, DLS and FTIR images of (a): CDs-BSA (b): NCDs-BSA and (c): SNCDs-BSA complexes.
Schematic illustration showing the structure of BSA and its four binding sites with CQDs.
Experimental design performed for)a): CDs-BSA complex, (b): NCDs-BSA complex and (c): SNCDs-BSA complex with different concentrations.
Experimental design results in the first and second stages of optimization for (a): CDs-BSA complex, (b): NCDs-BSA complex and (c): SNCDs-BSA complex.
ANOVA for the central composite design for R2: emission intensity of CDs-BSA complex.
ANOVA for the central composite design for R1: absorbance of CDs-BSA complex.
ANOVA for the central composite design for R2: emission intensity of NCDs-BSA complex.
ANOVA for the central composite design for R1: absorbance of NCDs-BSA complex.
ANOVA for the central composite design for R2: emission intensity of SNCDs-BSA complex.

ce:simple-para.ce:italic: ANOVA for the central composite design for R

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DLS and zeta-potential of CQDs-BSA complex.

head.ce:title: Development of various carbon nanoparticles and albumin complexes for potential theranostics applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are promising materials for many biomedical applications. However, their unique properties may be much enhanced by heteroatom doping and albumin decorating, which leads to improved functionality as reliable CQD-based simultaneous diagnosis and drug delivery systems, respectively. Therefore, in this study, three types of CQDs, with various doping conditions, namely pure carbon dot (CDs), N-doped CQD (NCDs), and S–N doped CQD (SNCDs), were functionalized over bovine serum albumin (BSA), a type of serum protein that is commonly used in drug delivery systems, and their binding was systematically optimized using the central composite design algorithm, by response surface methodology. Therefore three types of CQD-BSA complexes were obtained, namely CD-BSA, NCD-BSA, and SNCD-BSA. The statistical system was set to achieve the best results for both the absorbance of BSA, as well as the emission strength for each CQD-BSA complex. The structural changes of BSA have been investigated by the FTIR spectrum for various CQD-BSA complexes, indicating that the secondary structure and biological activity of BSA have not been influenced significantly, after incubation with different CQDs, except for a slight change in the case of SNCDs-BSA complex, inferring non-covalent interaction in some binding sites. The fluorescence intensity results also show that the formation of the complex did not reduce the emission of CQDs very much. Based on the results, the synthesized complexes can serve as suitable carriers for simultaneous drug delivery and diagnosis applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are quantum-sized carbon nanoparticles (less than10 nm), which due to their special properties, are promising materials for many various biomedical applications. Small size, biocompatibility, and stable chemical and fluorescent properties are among the valuable properties of CQDs [1]. In terms of biological diagnosis, fluorescence is still one of the most powerful and common tools [2]. Since being discovered in 2004 [3], fluorescent CQDs have shown great potential in different biological fields. In contrast to conventional heavy-metal semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs are superior in many aspects. For example, CQDs can be synthesized from low toxic organic compounds or natural materials, and hence have been recognized as an eco-friendly alternative for semiconductor quantum dots [4]. Furthermore, CQDs have emerged as promising alternatives to replace conventional fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots in various fields, especially in biomedical applications, such as biosensors, drug carriers, bioimaging agents, cancer therapy and gene transfections [5]. They have also attracted considerable attention as nano-heaters useful in photoacoustic imaging and PTT applications [6], due to possessing excellent photothermal conversion in the near-infrared (NIR) region.

Synthesis methods for CQDs can generally be classified into two main groups, i.e., top-down and bottom-up approaches [7]. Compared with top-down routes, the bottom-up approaches, including thermal pyrolysis or hydrothermal carbonization of the various precursors, have clear advantages in terms of providing the ability to adjust the composition and physical properties of the fabricated CQDs, by the careful selection of diverse organic precursors [8].

In general, CQDs are composed of the carbon core and the surface domain. The carbon core is generally characterized by sp2 carbon structure, similar to that of graphene, whereas the surface domain contains abundant functional groups such as N–H,–OH,–C = O, COOH, C–O, and C–N or polymer aggregates [9]. In terms of fluorescent properties, the photoluminescence (PL) CQDs, unlike many other inorganic nanoparticles, does not exhibit merely size-dependent PL, but it is related to the surface structures and chemical composition of the CQDs as well. Thus, heteroatom doping is a powerful strategy to modulate the properties of CQDs. Doping of CQDs refers to the addition of atomic impurities to their structure CQDs, which along with surface functionalization, i.e., the modification of the CQDs’ surface by molecules or polymers, are the two major techniques for enhancing some targeted characteristics of the CQD-based complex. However, doping of CQDs is not always feasible because the incorporation of some atoms into the structure of the CQDs is not energetically favorable owing to the high dissociation energy of covalent C–C bonds. Therefore, successful doping of CQDs is limited to several single elements such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphor, etc. or a combination of two or three of them [10].

Surface functionalization, as the other technique for modification of the properties of CQDs, influences the functional groups on the surface domain. The technique, not only can create or change the surface states but to endow CQDs with a variety of functionalities, which are achieved indirectly as a result of the existence of the new conjugated molecules or polymers as well, which can extend their application range, especially for various biological applications [11]. Furthermore, when CQD decorated BSA improves the quantum yield and the solubility of CQDs too [12]. As the surface charge of nanomaterials has significant effects on their biological response and their interaction with biological biomacromolecule, it is essential to reveal the mechanism of molecular interaction between different charged CQDs and the introduced proteins which can reflect the biological behavior of the resulting CQD-based complex [5].

Albumin is one of the most important proteins in blood plasma [13] and has been extensively explored as a drug delivery carrier, owing to its advantages, including high biocompatibility, non-antigenicity, and good biodegradability [14,15]. The binding properties of albumin provide it with a wide range of biochemical, clinical, and pharmaceutical applications [16]. Human albumin is the most abundant plasma protein with an average plasma concentration of 42.0 g/l (632.0 μM) and a circulatory half-life of 19 days [17]. Albumin's long circulatory half-life is the basis for many half-life extension strategies [18]. BSA is a type of serum protein that is extensively used and commercially available biomacromolecule in biological applications, for its capability of reducing nonspecific binding in an immunoassay [19]. It is a globular protein derived from cows, which possesses nitrogen elements, as well as a large number of amino and carboxyl groups [20]. Importantly, albumin can be inherently formulated the hydrophobic anticancer drugs for tumor-targeted delivery in its hydrophobic interiors, thereby improving pharmacokinetic profiles with an enhanced circulatory half-life of drugs [21]. As CQD-BSA complexes are a promising material to be used in medicine, mainly as drug carriers, in order to recognize and destroy cancer cells, it is important to understand how specific agents are delivered to tumor areas. Most nanoparticles are able to transport drugs to tumor cells in a passive manner, taking advantage of a selectively increased permeability and retention of tumor vessels, or by active transport through endocytic pathways. Molecules coated with various ligands bind to cell receptors and penetrate inside tumor cells by endocytosis, thus, delivering a higher concentration of a drug into them, without causing greater cytotoxicity to normal cells [22]. Synthesis of fluorescent albumin-CQD complexes, as simultaneous drug delivery and diagnosis systems, has also been considered.

Starting from 2014, these so-called theranostics agents were employed to diagnose various biomarkers or biochemical reactions, as well as to carry drugs with them and release them in the acidic environment of the tumor site. For instance, sorbitol-derived carbon dots for targeted delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) was used with folic acid (FA) as a carrier molecule due to its high expression in most cancer cells. Before the attachment of the DOX, the CQDs were conjugated with BSA to make the complex more biocompatible and able to hold a higher amount of DOX. The release profile of DOX was studied using standard statistical techniques. Cellular imaging was performed using fluorescence microscopy, which showed bright green-colored PL due to the internalization of CQDs with FA in HeLa cells [23]. Hyaluronic acid and CQDs functionalized human serum albumin nanoparticles were prepared and applied for bioimaging and targeted delivery of gemcitabine in pancreatic cancer cells. Due to the rapid metabolism and short half-life of gemcitabine, its efficacy reduces when administered in free form. However, when conjugated to a nano-formulation, the complex not only attained sustained release, but also showed to have a notable effect on the bioavailability of gemcitabine to cancer cells, as proved by in-vitro MTT assay. The presence of hyaluronic acid as the targeting part of the CQD-albumin complex was due to the better selectivity of the gemcitabine-containing complex to resistant pancreatic cancer cells, while CQDs exhibit excellent bioimaging potential [24]. In a more recent investigation, a stable drug delivery system of BSA@CQD-DOX was prepared, showing that the stability of the complex depends on its environment. The in-vitro release profile also showed that the cumulative release ratio at pH = 5.0 was 7.5 times higher than that at pH = 7.4 and side effects on the normal cells were significantly reduced over 72 h [25].

The design of theranostics agents, which have the ability to provide a variety of functional groups for protein, or enzyme immobilization is a critical step that can solve many challenges in the healthcare system. Hence, the development of novel CQDs-BSA complex elements, with enhanced properties and a vast variety of functional groups is essential for improving biomedical research and designing highly functional CQDs-based diagnostic and therapeutic complex elements.

Therefore, this study focuses on optimizing three different CQD-BSA complexes, based on the details of the influence of the interaction between differently doped CQDs and BSA, on the properties of the complex, to achieve and introduce reliable CQDs-based drug delivery and diagnosis systems, capable of loading various drugs and targeting different cancerous cell.

The pure CQDs (CDs), N-doped CQDs (NCDs) and S/N co-doped CQDs (SNCDs) carbon dots were synthesized using pyrolysis and hydrothermal techniques. A non-covalent process for functionalizing the three CQDs with BSA protein was systematically designed using statistical experimental design software, in a way that maintains both the properties of BSA, as well as the PL emission strength of each CQD at their best level.

The citric acid (MW: 192.1 g/mol), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Mw:40.0 g/mol), urea (MW: 60.0 g/mol), l-cysteine (MW:121/1 g/mol), and albumin were supplied by Merck (Germany). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) for particle size distribution and surface charge of particles (so-called zeta potential) were tested with HORIBA model SZ-100 produced in Kyoto, Japan was determined. DLS and zeta potential techniques were measured in 3 replications. PL investigation of CQDs, the PerkinElmer LS45, Massachusetts, USA, the optical characterization was performed. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed by Thermo Nicolet model NEXUS 470 made in Illinois, USA, and the chemical bond analysis was completed. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of CQDs was recorded by Rigaku, ultima Iv Tokyo, Japan. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using PHI 5000 Versaprobe, Physical Electronics, Minnesota, USA and bright-field high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was conducted to analyze the morphology and particle size of the samples. The specimens were highly diluted in distilled water. The suspension was then homogenized in an ultrasonic bath for 5 min.

Pure CDs were synthesized using a simple pyrolysis method [26]. Briefly, 1.0 g of citric acid as carbon precursor was placed in a glass beaker and heated to 160 °C for 55 min and then neutralized to pH of 7 using 1 M NaOH solution, under continuous stirring. The achieved light-yellow solution was centrifuged (20K rpm for 20 min) to separate the large particles and then it was filtered to obtain the pure CDs. Finally, the CDs solution was freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder.

In this method, 1.0 gr of citric acid and 1.0 gr urea were dissolved in 50 ml distilled water under continuous stirring to obtain a clear solution [27]. Then the solution was moved into a Teflon-lined stainless steel hydrothermal reactor (150 ml) and was heated for 8 h in the oven at 180 °C. The resulting solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature, providing a bright green solution and was then neutralized to a pH of 7 using 1 M NaOH solution. The solution was centrifuged (20K rpm for 30 min) to separate the large particles and then it was filtered and freeze-dried to obtain NCDs powder.

SNCDs were prepared in accordance with previously described methods with minor modifications [28]. Citric acid monohydrate (2.0 g) and l-cysteine (1.0 g) were well dissolved in 5 ml of distilled water and were put to evaporated at 70 °C for 12 h. The resultant thick syrup was heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-lined reactor at 200 °C for 3 h. The obtained was neutralized to pH 7.0 and diluted to 100 ml using distilled water. It was then centrifuged and freeze-dried to provide SNCDs powder.

To design the experiments, data modelling and analysis were performed using the response surface methodology over the central composite design algorithm in the Design-Expert®. Based on the preliminary studies, the concentration of carbon nanoparticles and albumin were introduced to the software as the main independent variables. The protein absorbance (R1), as a feature that shows how intact the second structure of the protein has remained, and the PL intensity of the complex(R2), as a feature that shows the fluorescence emission capability of the complex, were defined as the dependent response variables for each CQD-BSA complex. The variables R1 and R2 are the two major factors that are necessary to be used for a theranostics agent with drug delivery and diagnosis properties. As brought in Table 1 , two influencing experimental factors were defined at five coded values (−α, −1, 0, 1, +α). Based on the employed method, having two influencing experimental factors, and five replications at the center point, a total of 13 experimental runs were proposed for each CQD. The analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed to study the observations and proceed to the second optimization stage.

To obtain the concentration range for each CQD, a specific initial concentration range, based on the available literature on the topic, was first considered from 1 to 5 mg/ml for the three CQDs. After measuring their PL, it was observed that the doped CQDs, i.e. the NCDs and SNCDs show better PL at lower concentrations. Hence, stepwise diluting was adjusted to the lowest concentrations of the initial stock solution, in a way that their PL is retained and not significantly lowered as the result of the dilution.

In the ANOVA of the data, when the p-value is lower than 0.05, the values are considered significant. The quality of the fitting polynomial model is also described by the correlation coefficient value (R2). Furthermore, there are three other indicators, which show the significance and adequacy of the suggested model. These indicators include the model's Fisher variation ratio (F-value), which is a statistically valid measure of how well the factors describe the variation in the data around its mean, the probability value (Prob > F), and adequate precision [26]. Finally, the three-dimensional (3D) response surface analysis of the dependent and independent variables was obtained to provide the optimal region for the independent variables for each CQD. According to the results of the first optimization stage, the range of the factor changes was assigned, for the second optimization stage, described below.

The interaction of the CQDs with BSA was carried out according to the designed experiments. 30.0 mg of BSA was dissolved in 10 ml of water to make an aqueous stock solution (3000 ppm). CQDs-BSA complex incubation was performed at room temperature, for 2 h [29], based on the concentration values, as suggested by the statistical evaluation software Design-Expert to achieve optimized composition. The statistical system was set to achieve the best results for both the absorbance of each CQDs-BSA complex and its emission intensity, as measures to show the minimum structural change of BSA and PL capability of CQDs, respectively, after incubation.

Due to the maximum absorbance of BSA at 280 nm wavelength, the absorbance of the samples was measured at this wavelength. For that, 2 ml of the CQDs-BSA complex was prepared for each run. The samples and the blank sample were prepared using deionized water at room temperature (pH 7.4) and data recording was done under identical conditions after incubation with all CQDs at different runs, providing R1, as to be the first dependent variable, showing the activity of BSA. In the final stage, the absorbance of the complex was divided by the absolute absorbance of BSA at that concentration to provide the complex's absorbance in percentage (%).

The emission intensities of CQDs were recorded initially before and after incubation at each run. The initial emission of each CQD before incubation was recorded as the absolute emission intensity; and the intensity of the emission after incubation, as the current emission. R2, as to be the second dependent variable, is obtained by dividing the current emission intensity by the absolute emission intensity, in percentage (%). The assessment of PL intensity at each run was performed using the image-processing software ImageJ.

In the study of the size and size distribution of the CQDs, TEM and DLS techniques were employed. Pure CDs obtained by pyrolysis of citric acid represent a size distribution of 8.2 ± 3.8 nm, according to HR-TEM and DLS results(Fig. 1 a and b, respectively). In Fig. 1b, the HR-TEM image of NCDs shows a rather uniform distribution of diameter 4.2 ± 1.8, as confirmed by DLS results, shown in Fig. 1c. The HR-TEM image of SNCDs in Fig. 1e clearly shows the well dispersed and homogenous size distribution of the particles with the majority having a diameter of 6.3 ± 0.4 nm, which agrees with DLS results in Fig. 1f. The synthesized CQDs are majorly below 10 nm in diameter, with significant sp2 carbon structure, as confirmed by XPS and XRD results, in the following sections. DLS and TEM reports of BSA size distribution show an average diameter of about 214.0 ± 12.7 nm, as presented in Fig. 1g and h. The zeta-potential results indicate that the pure CDs have a negative surface charge of −19.1 ± 1.3 mV, whereas it is equal to-22.8 ± 2.0 mV for NCDs. The high negative charge on the surface of these two CQDs is due to the existence of a higher amount of the carboxyl groups, compared to SNCDs, which have only −0.3 ± 0.1 mV of negative charge on their surface. BSA also shows a negative surface charge of −20.0 ± 2.0 mV.

PL properties of CQDs are important, especially for biological imaging and biosensing, which occur due to the presence of n→π* electron transitions associated with the CO functional group. In their PL characteristics, a maximum absorption wavelength of 360 nm and emission of 450 nm and 420 nm, for pure CDs and NCDs, respectively (Fig. 2 a and b) [10]. PL characteristics properties for SNCDs (Fig. 2c) show two maximum absorptions of 207 and 350 nm and emission of 500 nm. At an excitation of 209 nm, no clear PL signal appears because of the π→π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains, indicating the existence of aromatic heterocycles [30].

The XRD pattern of the CDs shows the crystal structure (002) corresponding to the structure of hexagonal sp2, at an angle of 2θ in the range of 20–30°. Based on the data analysis, the distance between the carbon plates or the height of the CDs at an angle of 25° was 0.36 nm (Fig. 2a). Due to the presence of oxygenated functional groups, it is slightly longer than the distance of graphene plates (0.34 nm) [31]. X-ray diffraction analysis for NCDs shows that the crystal plates (002) and (100) in samples can be detected at angles 2θ between 20 to 30 and 40–45°, respectively (Fig. 2b). The XRD diagram shows crystal plates in the SNCDs sample, at an angle of 2θ equal to about 30° (Fig. 2c).

The characteristic chemical bond absorption pattern of the CQDs was obtained using the FTIR spectrum. It confirms that in pure CDs (Fig. 2a), C–O stretching vibration between 1310 and 1250 cm−1 and aromatic CC bonds stretching and bending frequencies at about 1560 and 842 cm−1 are significant. C–H bending is also observed at 1377 cm−1. In the NCDs, a broad stretching bond at 1060 cm−1 is observed that is related to the carboxylic bonds and aromatic CC can be identified at about 1450 cm−1 and amide bonds between 1600 and 1700 cm−1. Amine functional groups are those vibrations ranging from 3485 to 3560 cm−1 wavenumbers (Fig. 2b). In SNCDs, S–CN, N–CO carbonyl amide stretchings, are observed in the ranges of 2175–2140 cm−1 and 1800-1600 cm−1, respectively. The sp3 bonds of carbon with nitrogen, oxygen and sulfur, as to be the C–N, C–O and C–S, as well as the CS bond can be distinguished in 1379, 1159 and 717 cm−1, and 1072 cm−1 respectively (Fig. 2c) [28]. The FTIR peaks of pure BSA at 3283, 2957, 1645, and 1531 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of –OH, amide (mainly –NH stretching vibration), amide I (mainly CO stretching vibrations), and amide II (the coupling of bending vibration of N–H and stretching vibration of C–N) bonds, as shown in Fig. 2d. Due to the aromatic amino acids (Trp, Tyr, and Phe) and backbone, BSA has two absorbance peaks at 275 and 211 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, which originate from the n→π* transition and peptide bonds of BSA, respectively [32].

The elemental composition and the chemical states of CQDs were provided by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The XPS survey data for the CDs (Fig. 3 a) contains major peaks at around 283.3 and 530.6 eV that correspond to C1s (C–C aromatic) and O1s (C–O), respectively, as confirmed by FTIR findings for CDs. For the NCDs (Fig. 3b), the peaks at around 283.3–286.5, 530.3–533.9 and 398.3–399.7 eV correspond to C1s, O1s, and N1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum indicates that the NCD samples originating from urea contain three peaks at binding energies of 283.3, 284.7 and 286.5 eV, which correspond to C–C sp2, C–C sp3 and CO bonds respectively [1], with the highest concentration of about 64% belonging to the sp2 C–C aromatic, as admitted by FTIR results. The high-resolution spectra of N1s reveal the presence of two types of C–N bond configurations. The peaks at 398.3 and 399.7 indicate the pyridinic-N, and pyrrolic-N respectively [33].

In the case of SNCD, the full XPS spectrum (Fig. 3c) reveals the existence of carbon (∼56.1%), oxygen (∼39.7%), nitrogen (∼1.7%), and sulfur (∼2.5%) bonds. The high-resolution C1s, O1s, N1s and S2p spectrum of SNCDs are demonstrated. In SNCDs XPS analysis, the C1s spectrum can be resolved as sp2 and sp3 C–C bonds at 283. 5 and 284.8 eV, respectively, and CO at 286.6 eV. The N1s spectrum has two peaks at 398.7 eV and 396.2 eV, which could be attributed to pyrrolic and pyridinic C–N bonds, respectively. The S2p spectrum shows two peaks at 162.5 and 165.2 eV related to C–S–C and S2p 1/2, respectively. The C, O, N and S bonds in the SNCDs are originated from citric acid and l-cysteine precursors, as indicated by FTIR and XPS results, which leads to excellent water solubility and promising optical properties of the nanostructure [34].

As already mentioned, the incubation between the different CQDs and BSA were evaluated based on two independent factors, that is, the concentration of each nanoparticle and the BSA concentration. At the first optimization step, 13 experiments were assigned for each CQD and the two major responses, namely, the absorbance (R1) and the fluorescence emission intensity (R2) of each synthesized complex, were assigned to be minimized and maximized, respectively. In the second step of the optimization, the obtained experimental results of the first step of the optimization are statistically analyzed by the system, in a way that at least 95% confidence is achieved for 99% of the populations of the variables, which is limited to two independent variables here (i.e., a two-sided problem). According to the results of the second analysis, the best incubation conditions are introduced for each CQDs-BSA complex, at CDs, NCDs, SNCDs and BSA concentrations of 1.0 mg/ml, 115.0 μg/ml, 23.0 μg/ml and 2.0 mg/ml concentration respectively, as provided in Table 2 .

The PL of BSA originates from tryptophan, which can be significantly affected by some molecules and is sensitive to its local surroundings [5]. So fluorescent methods were applied to study the PL quenching efficiency and mechanism of BSA by three CQDs. These results suggest that CDs, NCDs, and SNCDs can effectively bind to BSA and they have little effect on the microenvironment of tryptophan residue. On the other hand, the effect of BSA on the PL shift of CQDs is also studied, and interaction with BSA is evidenced by a slight increase in PL in some cases. This indicates that BSA has little effect on the CQDs’ emissions. The significance of each term is determined by the p-value. The values of p < 0.05 indicate the importance of the term. The less than 0.0001 p-values imply that the model term is significant, whereas all values greater than 1, designate the corresponding model terms not to be significant.

According to the ANOVA (Table 4 ) for absorbance of CDs-BSA complex and based on the p-value analysis, the interaction of the CDs and the BSA concentration was not significant and the concentration of BSA is the influential factor in the absorbance of the complex. Fig. 4b, shows the effect of CDs and BSA concentration on the absorbance, confirming that the absorbance of the complex depends more on the BSA concentration rather than the CDs concentration. The adjusted-R2 and R2 values were 97.1% and 98.3%, respectively.

According to the ANOVA (Table 6 ) for absorbance of NCDs-BSA complex and based on p-values, all the first- and second-order, and the simultaneous interactions of both factors, i.e., the NCDs and BSA concentrations were significant in absorbance. Fig. 5b, shows the effect of NCDs and BSA concentration on absorbance, indicating that the absorbance depends on the NCDs and BSA concentration, increased by increasing this variable concentration. The adjusted-R2 and R2 values were both 99.9%.

According to the ANOVA (Table 8 ) for emission intensity of SNCDs-BSA complex and based on p-values, all the first-order and simultaneous interactions of both factors, i.e., the SNCDs and BSA concentration were significantly influential on the absorbance of the complex. Fig. 6b also indicated that the absorbance depends directly on both BSA and SNCDs concentrations. The values of adjusted-R2 and R2 values were 99.7% and 99.8%, respectively.

Using the achieved optimum synthesis conditions, the CQD-BSA complexes were made and characterized as follows The DLS results of the CDs-BSA complex show that the average diameter of the synthesized complex is about 250.0 ± 65.1 nm, the diagram (Fig. 7 a). The NCDs-BSA complex shows that the average diameter of the synthesized complex of two sizes is about 300.0 ± 41.2 nm (Fig. 7b) and the SNCDs-BSA complex shows that the average diameter of the synthesized complex of two sizes is about 279.0 ± 10.9 (Fig. 7c). The zeta-potential results indicated that the CDs-BSA complex has a surface charge of −30.0 mV, due to the negative charge of BSA, the surface load of the complex has increased. The NCDs-BSA complex surface charge is −20.0 mV and the SNCDs-BSA complex its surface charge is −31.0 m.

In order to compare the characteristic absorptions of the chemical bonds in the CDs, NCDs, SNCDs and the CDs-BSA complex, NCDs-BSA complex and SNCDs-BSA complex, FTIR spectra of the samples were obtained. FTIR spectroscopy is another valuable method to monitor changes in the secondary structure of proteins and their interactions with other molecules. FTIR spectra can reflect the change in the three-dimensional structure of BSA and the conformation of secondary structures for the CQDs-BSA complex. In FTIR spectra, the frequencies of bands due to amide I and amide II vibrations are sensitive to the change in the secondary structure of BSA. The amide I band occurs in the region 1600-1700 cm−1 (mainly CO stretch) and the amide II peak occurs in the region 1500 cm−1 (C–N stretch coupled with N–H bending mode). The results of the FTIR of the CDs-BSA complex show that after the loading of BSA, the peak of BSA nanoparticles has moved from 1513 cm−1 to 1531 cm−1 and in the SNCDs-BSA complex from 1731 cm−1 to 1789 cm−1 (Fig. 7a and c). This may be due to the adsorption of amino groups of BSA nanoparticles on the surface of the complex. The result of FTIR spectra shows that the bands of amide I and amide II do not show any obvious shift and the secondary structure of BSA has not changed after incubation with various CQDs.

The complexes were fabricated based on the proposed optimum synthesis conditions. Later the performance of the complexes was studied to investigate their stability. R1, R2 and zeta potential of the complexes were measured over 7-day, 14-day and 45-day intervals after incubation. According to the results in Table 9 , although an initial insignificant decrease was observed in the first few days, the complexes retained their properties then after.

Fluorescence nanoparticles play an important role in biomedical applications, as agents for biosensing, molecule tracking, etc. Therefore, studies focusing on the interaction between them and other biological molecules, ions, ligands, and various proteins or drugs are vital in understanding the mechanism of their performance in a biological environment. Fluorescence quenching or the shifting of the emission wavelength serves as a sensitive indicator for the alterations of the fluorophore particles or structural changes in proteins or particles during the binding or addition of foreign molecules [29]. In fluorescence nanoparticles, doping can generally increase photoabsorption, and sometimes extend the spectral response of CQDs to red wavelength and even to near-infrared wavelengths by generating intragap states. In this study, for instance, the PL intensity of SNCDs is much stronger than CDs and NCDs, which may be associated with the co-doping of sulfur and nitrogen into their structure [10]. However, the emission intensity of CQDs depends more on alternations in the functional groups on their surface. The slight changes in emission intensity, after the formation of the complex, in the case of CDs and SNCDs, as provided in Table 9, therefore, indicate that the interaction of CQDs and BSA has not significantly influenced their chemical structure and the distribution of electrons on their surface. However, in the case of the NCDs-BSA complex, the formation of the complex resulted in an initial significant increase in the PL emission of the complex. This phenomenon is attributed to the minimization of the reabsorption effect by separating NCDs from each other. The reabsorption effect occurs when CQDs aggregate and as a result, the emitted PL of a CQD is absorbed by its neighbour. Hence, the separation of CQDs through embedding them in polymers and/or biological matrices like protein, like in the case of NCDs-BSA complex, can significantly enhance their PL [35]. However, the initial increase was reduced in the first few days after the complex was synthesized, as shown in Table 9, reaching rather stable conditions over the following days after.

The size of the complexes, together with the FTIR studies, proves a non-covalent incubation of the particles, in which CQDs are adsorbed onto the surface of BSA, through electrostatic or hydrogen bonding. Based on the structural analysis, four CQD binding sites were identified in the BSA structure. Binding site 1, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 8 , resides in the central cavity of the BSA. Binding sites 2 to 4 are situated at the interface of two subdomains in the neighbourhood. Site 2 resides at the interface between subdomains IA and IB, whereas site 3 resides at the interface between subdomains IIA and IIB, and site 4 resides at the interface between subdomains IIIA and IIIB, which contain substitutions of amino acids, that create interactions across inter-domain boundaries within the BSA. Depending on the pH of the solution, different sites are more likely to host CQDsas their binding location. At pH 6.0, CQDs tend more to bind at the binding sites 2 to 4, due to the electrostatic interactions formed by the residues with specific positive charges. At pH 7.4, CQDs can bind to BSA in both the central cavity and the interface between neighbourhood subdomains, i.e. all the binding sites tribute to the surface adsorption. However, at pH 9.0 CQDs would prefer to bind with BSA in its central cavity (binding site 1), due to the favorable hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions which lead to a higher binding capacity [36].

However, in the case of the SNCDs-BSA complex, FTIR studies show a rather more significant change in the complex after incubation. This finding is admitted by the decrease observed in the absorbance of the complex to 50% after incubation, as provided in Table 9, and by the significant change in the Zeta potential of the SNCDs from around zero to −31.0. The observation infers a change in the three-dimensional structure of BSA and the conformation of secondary structures for the SNCDs-BSA complex, implying non-covalent binding in some binding sites between SNCDs and BSA.

In this investigation, three types of CQDs were synthesized by different techniques, precursors and surface properties, namely pure CDs, single-heteroatom doped NCDs and double-heteroatom doped SNCDs. The CQDs were studied to optimize their incubation conditions to interact with BSA and form CQD-BSA complexes for targeted drug delivery and simultaneous diagnosis or molecule tracking applications. The proposed CQD-BSA complexes have the potential ability to carry various drugs, based on the affinities between the interaction sites of the participating nanostructures and various drugs.

Response surface methodology over the central composite design algorithm was used to figure out the optimal responses within the specified ranges of the variable factors protein absorbance (R1) and fluorescence intensity (R2) of the complex were assigned as the two dependent response variables for each CQD-BSA complex. Based on the employed method, having two influencing experimental factors, and five replications at the center point, a total of 13 experimental runs were proposed for each CQD. The analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed to study the observations and proceed to the second stage. The statistical system was set to achieve the best results for both the absorbance of each CQDs-BSA complex and its emission intensity, as measures to show the minimum structural change of BSA and PL capability of CQDs, respectively, after incubation.

Absorbance studies, together with FTIR spectrum investigation for each complex indicate that the secondary structure and biological activity of BSA have not been influenced significantly, after incubation with different CQDs, proving a non-covalent incubation of the particles, in which CQDs are adsorbed onto the surface of BSA, through electrostatic or hydrogen bonding. However, in the case of the SNCDs-BSA complex, FTIR studies show a rather more significant change in the complex after incubation. This finding is admitted by the decrease observed in the absorbance of the complex to 50% after incubation, inferring some slight changes in the three-dimensional structure of BSA and the conformation of secondary structures for the SNCDs-BSA complex. The observation implies a non-covalent binding in some binding sites between SNCDs and BSA. However, as concluded from the retained PL emission of the complex, the majority of the surface functional groups of the CQD are not influenced and the proposed CQD-based complexes can serve as reliable carriers for drug delivery, with fluorescent properties, enabling molecule tracking and optical sensing for the developed complexes, which showed to maintain their desired properties for at least 45 days.

M.Y. performed the experiments and analyzed the results with the software and contributed in writing the manuscript. M.R. defined and conducted the project, contributed in writing and revising the manuscript, and in analysis of the results. H.K. cooperated in defining and conducting the project, revising the manuscript and analysis of the results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.
